skill.name: generating-output-styles
skill.description: Creates custom Claude Code output styles with valid frontmatter, clear behavior instructions, and correct placement guidance.
skill.summary: Generate Claude Code output styles that adjust response tone, structure, and role behavior.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, weak cryptography, network, and hidden-file patterns, but the command and cryptography matches are Markdown syntax or documentation examples. The confirmed risk is that the skill helps create persistent output-style files that modify Claude Code system-prompt behavior, including user-level files under a hidden Claude directory.

medium_findings.title: Persistent System Prompt Customization
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs creation of output-style Markdown files that modify Claude Code behavior and can be saved at user or project scope. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can persistently alter assistant behavior if a user creates unsafe style instructions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly state that output styles modify Claude Code system prompts and are saved in recognized directories. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because the skill also states that styles cannot override tool permissions or security restrictions.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Matches Are Markdown Syntax
low_findings.description: The reported external command findings are inline code spans, slash-command documentation, or fenced Markdown examples. I did not find evidence of Ruby backtick execution or shell command invocation in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation using backticks for commands, paths, field names, and examples. No executable script, subprocess call, or command interpolation is present.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography findings occur on lines that discuss descriptions, frontmatter fields, and output-style examples. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, encryption, hashing code, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context contains prose and YAML frontmatter names, not cryptographic APIs or algorithms. The scanner appears to have matched short substrings inside ordinary documentation.

low_findings.title: Documentation URL Does Not Exfiltrate Data
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a documentation pointer to Claude Code documentation. There is no network request code, no secret collection, and no instruction to send local data to the URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Only a static documentation URL is present at the cited line. The skill files contain no fetch, curl, webhook, or other network execution mechanism.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Claude Configuration Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents saving output-style files under a hidden Claude directory and project-level Claude directory. This is expected for Claude Code configuration, but it can persist behavior changes beyond a single prompt.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The locations directly reference hidden or project-level Claude output-style paths. The pattern is a legitimate configuration mechanism, not an attempt to hide unrelated files.

content.value_statement: Teams often need Claude Code to respond in a specific role, tone, or structure. This skill helps create valid output-style files with clear instructions and correct placement guidance.
actual_capabilities: Generates Markdown output-style files with YAML frontmatter.
Defines names, descriptions, and keep-coding-instructions settings.
Explains user-level and project-level output-style placement.
Creates behavior instructions for tone, role, and response structure.
Provides examples for technical writing and concise response styles.
Guides testing through the Claude Code output-style menu.
limitations: Does not create a separate agent with independent tools or model settings.
Cannot override Claude Code tool permissions or security restrictions.
Requires user review before saving persistent style instructions.
Depends on Claude Code support for output-style files and settings.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Response Style
use_cases.description: Create a project-level output style that keeps responses consistent across repeated development tasks.

use_cases.title: Build a Teaching Mode
use_cases.description: Create an educational style that keeps coding guidance while adding explanations and learning prompts.

use_cases.title: Create Domain-Specific Assistance
use_cases.description: Define a custom role for documentation, product writing, or support workflows inside Claude Code.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Style
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a simple response style.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code output style named Concise Reviewer. It should keep coding instructions and make responses brief, direct, and focused on risks.

prompt_templates.title: Add Teaching Behavior
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate style that changes explanation depth.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an output style for mentoring junior developers. Keep coding instructions, add short explanations before major changes, and include one learning question when useful.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Non-Coding Role
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced style for work outside software engineering.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an output style for a product requirements analyst. Do not keep coding instructions. Focus on clarifying goals, constraints, acceptance criteria, and open questions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Existing Style
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of persistent prompt behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this output-style draft for clear frontmatter, safe instructions, and conflicts with Claude Code security rules. Suggest precise edits before I save it.

output_examples.input: User asks for a technical writer output style.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a named style with a user-facing description, keeps coding instructions enabled, and adds documentation-focused response behavior.

output_examples.input: User asks where to save a style for one repository.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends the project output-styles directory and explains that project scope avoids changing behavior across all repositories.

output_examples.input: User asks whether an output style can bypass permissions.
output_examples.output: The skill states that output styles change response behavior but cannot override tool permissions or security restrictions.

best_practices: Review style instructions before saving them to a user-level directory.
Use keep-coding-instructions only when the style should preserve software engineering guidance.
Test each style with representative tasks before sharing it with a team.
anti_patterns: Do not use output styles to weaken security rules or tool permission checks.
Do not save experimental styles globally when only one project needs them.
Do not write vague role instructions that conflict with the intended workflow.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates Claude Code output-style Markdown files with frontmatter and behavior instructions.

faq.question: Does it change Claude Code permissions?
faq.answer: No. Output styles can change response behavior, but they cannot override tool permissions or security restrictions.

faq.question: Where can output styles be saved?
faq.answer: They can be saved at user level or project level in recognized Claude Code output-style directories.

faq.question: When should keep-coding-instructions be true?
faq.answer: Use true when the style should keep Claude Code software engineering guidance and add behavior on top.

faq.question: Is this safe for shared repositories?
faq.answer: It can be safe after review. Teams should inspect style instructions before committing them to a repository.

faq.question: Can it create styles for non-coding work?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill can define roles for writing, analysis, documentation, and other workflows.
